provenance.source_corpus: Deer browsing outweighs the effects of site preparation and mechanical release on balsam fir seedlings performance: Implications to forest management

source_spans.text: Deer browsing on surrounding species modifies vegetation structure and composition towards more open environments, improving light conditions for the planted seedlings.
Cumulative browsing on terminal shoot (ranging from 0 to 4 browsing events) had a negative eﬀect on height (F 1,162 = 3.8, p = 0.05) and diameter (F 1,163 = 5.3, p = 0.02).
Cumulative browsing on terminal shoot (ranging from 0 to 4 browsing events) had a negative eﬀect on height (F 1,162 = 3.8, p = 0.05) and diameter (F 1,163 = 5.3, p = 0.02).
Deer browsing on surrounding species modifies vegetation structure and composition towards more open environments, improving light conditions for the planted seedlings.

The cover and modal height of large herbaceous species decreased from 35 ± 2% and 94.7 ± 3.0 cm in fenced plots to 18 ± 1% and 62.6 ± 1.4 cm in unfenced plots (F 1,12 = 21.0; p < 0.01 and F1,12=43.8, p < 0.01, respectively).
Graminoids positively responded to the soil disturbance, probably as a result of the dispersing and fragmenting of rhizomes
Graminoids positively responded to the soil disturbance, probably as a result of the dispersing and fragmenting of rhizomes

Deer exclusion increased the cover of deciduous species in unprepared plots (site preparation × deer exclusion: F 2,12 = 3.75, p = 0.05; no deer: 18.6 ± 4.5%; with deer: 9.3 ± 2.2%, t 8.7 = −3.3; p = 0.01)

Deer exclusion increased the cover of deciduous species in unprepared plots (site preparation × deer exclusion: F 2,12 = 3.75, p = 0.05; no deer: 18.6 ± 4.5%; with deer: 9.3 ± 2.2%, t 8.7 = −3.3; p = 0.01)
Survival ... tended to be lower when deer were excluded (56.0 ± 5.4%; mean ± standard error) compared to control conditions (65.7 ± 4.1%) (F 1,12 = 3.51, p = 0.09; Fig. 2).

balsam ﬁr seedlings submitted to mechanical release tended to have a larger diameter (2.4 ± 0.1 cm vs. 1.6 ± 0.1 cm) and a smaller h:d ratio (50 ± 1 vs. 59 ± 1) than seedlings that had not been released ( Table 1).
